refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies a specific DP) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design of each capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent designated work zones (operational areas, restricted zones, safety perimeters) in the worksite model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and retrieve worksite model data for access by planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The candidate FR is not atomic; it has been decomposed into four atomic sub-FRs, each addressing a single functional obligation without internal 'and'/'or' logic.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign zone-level feature inventories (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; maintain zone-feature mappings in a relational schema; provide zone-based query API.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a voxel grid with occupancy and semantic labels; update grid incrementally as new observations arrive; convert grid to feature primitives (planes, edges, clusters) on demand for planning queries.

DP4: Acquire raw sensor streams (camera, lidar, IMU) and store as time-indexed data lake; defer feature extraction and representation to query time; provide lazy evaluation of spatial models on consumer request.

DP5: Integrate worksite safety plan and operational policies as constraints into the spatial model; represent features with associated policy rules (e.g., exclusion zones, speed limits, access permissions); expose constrained feature set to planning/execution via policy-aware query interface.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP5 (integrated): Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; represent as a structured spatial graph with feature geometry, classification, and safety/policy attributes; maintain bidirectional traceability between features and worksite safety plan constraints; expose via a policy-aware query interface that returns feature sets filtered and annotated according to operational policies and acceptance criteria.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor fusion logic and spatial representation format (mitigated by defining DP at logical level, deferring IM choice of fusion algorithm and graph structure).
Dependency on worksite safety plan schema and policy language (acceptable: policies are external inputs; DP must accommodate them but not define them).
Coupling between feature acquisition rate and planning/execution query latency (mitigated by decoupling acquisition from query via asynchronous update and caching).
coupling_check.notes: DP1+DP5 avoids premature commitment to specific sensor modalities, fusion algorithms, or graph database implementations. The logical separation of acquisition, representation, and policy annotation supports independent evolution of each concern. Coupling to external safety plan is intentional and necessary; it is managed via a well-defined policy schema boundary.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Multi-modal sensor data is available and can be synchronized to a common coordinate frame. Risk: If sensors are asynchronous or poorly calibrated, fusion quality degrades; mitigation: define sensor synchronization and calibration acceptance criteria early.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable or change infrequently. Risk: Frequent policy updates may require re-annotation of features; mitigation: design policy schema to be versioned and support incremental updates.

Assumption: Feature extraction and classification can be automated or semi-automated with acceptable accuracy. Risk: Manual annotation bottleneck or misclassification errors propagate to planning/execution; mitigation: define feature classification taxonomy and acceptance thresholds; implement human-in-the-loop validation.

Assumption: Planning and execution functions can tolerate model latency (e.g., features acquired at t0 available for query at t0+Δt). Risk: If Δt is large, model becomes stale for dynamic worksites; mitigation: define acceptable model age and refresh frequency.

Assumption: Spatial graph representation is sufficient for all downstream planning/execution use cases. Risk: Some use cases may require richer representations (e.g., temporal dynamics, probabilistic uncertainty); mitigation: design query interface to support extensible feature attributes.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP1+DP5 to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Spatial acquisition and fusion (DP1 core), (2) Feature representation and storage (DP1 realization), (3) Policy binding and constraint annotation (DP5), (4) Query interface and consumer adaptation (DP1 interface). Define acceptance criteria for each: e.g., 'spatial model is generated within T seconds of final sensor observation' and 'all features are tagged with applicable safety constraints from the worksite plan.' Clarify the boundary between 'feature' (geometry + classification) and 'constraint' (policy rule) to avoid conflating representation with policy logic. Consider whether the spatial graph should be immutable (snapshot) or mutable (incremental updates), as this affects coupling to planning/execution latency requirements.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for acquiring multi-modal spatial feature data, fusing sensor inputs into a unified coordinate frame, maintaining a structured spatial graph with feature geometry/classification/safety attributes, enforcing bidirectional traceability to worksite safety plan constraints, and exposing a policy-aware query interface for feature retrieval filtered by operational policies and acceptance criteria.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound multi-modal sensor streams (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) from Vehicle; raw spatial observations and feature detections.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound policy-aware feature queries and filtered feature sets to Teleoperation and Vehicle for mission planning; inbound task context and operational policy updates from Teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound alerts when spatial features violate safety plan constraints or acceptance criteria; traceability annotations linking detected violations to worksite safety plan.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Potential remote synchronization of spatial graph state and safety plan constraints if distributed architecture required; bidirectional consistency maintenance.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. Safety Services is the natural home for spatial feature fusion, safety attribute management, constraint traceability, and policy-aware filtering. The DP's core logic (sensor fusion, graph representation, safety constraint binding, policy-filtered queries) aligns directly with safety-critical decision support. Minor diffusion: raw telemetry ingestion depends on Vehicle/Telemetry interface; query results consumed by Teleoperation/Vehicle for planning. This is acceptable boundary diffusion (data flow, not logic duplication).

Poor containment. Vehicle is primarily a hardware/actuation platform. Placing spatial fusion, graph maintenance, and policy-aware query logic in Vehicle would scatter safety-critical reasoning across the platform layer. Sensor acquisition would be co-located, but constraint traceability, policy filtering, and safety attribute management would diffuse to Teleoperation and Safety Services. Violates separation of concerns.

Weak containment. Teleoperation is a command/control interface for human operators. While it consumes policy-aware feature queries, it should not own the spatial fusion logic, graph representation, or constraint traceability machinery. Placing DP here would couple operator interface tightly to safety-critical data management, making testing and reuse difficult. Safety logic would diffuse to Safety Services anyway.

Moderate containment. NGHE Solution is a top-level system integrator. It could theoretically orchestrate spatial fusion and safety services, but doing so would make NGHE responsible for low-level sensor fusion, graph algorithms, and constraint binding—tasks better owned by a dedicated Safety Services module. Diffusion would be high across Vehicle (telemetry source), Teleoperation (query consumer), and Safety Services (constraint repository). Better as a consumer of Safety Services outputs.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most cohesive and least diffuse boundary for the integrated DP. It consolidates all spatial feature acquisition, fusion, graph representation, safety attribute binding, constraint traceability, and policy-aware query logic into a single, testable, reusable module. External dependencies are limited to inbound Telemetry (sensor data), outbound Tasking (query results), SafetyAlert (constraint violations), and optional Network Link (distributed sync). This boundary respects the principle that safety-critical reasoning should be centralized and independently verifiable.
containment_risks: Risk: Telemetry interface may carry high-bandwidth multi-modal streams; Safety Services must implement efficient buffering and prioritization to avoid bottlenecks.
Risk: Spatial graph representation and constraint traceability may grow large in complex worksites; memory and query latency must be monitored and optimized.
Risk: Policy updates (via Tasking inbound) must be validated and applied atomically to avoid inconsistent feature filtering; synchronization logic required.
Risk: Bidirectional traceability between features and safety plan constraints requires careful data structure design to avoid circular dependencies or stale references.

Risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation modules attempt to cache or replicate spatial features locally for performance, diffusion and consistency issues will arise; strict ownership of the spatial graph by Safety Services is critical.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') maps directly to Safety Services IM boundary. Acceptance criteria are verifiable: (1) worksite model generation can be tested via sensor simulation and fusion output validation; (2) availability to planning/execution can be verified through interface contract testing (Tasking outbound); (3) feature set agreement can be validated against worksite safety plan constraints via traceability audit. The policy-aware query interface provides an explicit verification point for filtering correctness.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. Safety Services is unambiguously assigned ownership of spatial feature capture, fusion, representation, and policy-aware exposure. The IM boundary scope explicitly lists all responsibilities. External interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert out, Network Link optional) are clearly defined. No ambiguity about which module owns the spatial graph or constraint traceability logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The integrated DP (DP1 + DP5) comprehensively addresses the FR. DP1 (multi-modal sensing + unified coordinate frame fusion) satisfies 'capture'; DP5 (structured spatial graph + feature geometry/classification/safety attributes + bidirectional traceability + policy-aware query interface) satisfies 'represent in machine-readable model'. The DP explicitly binds safety plan constraints and operational policies to feature representation, enabling the acceptance criterion that the model is available to planning/execution functions. No functional gap identified.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry interface diffusion: Raw multi-modal sensor streams (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) originate in Vehicle module. Safety Services depends on Vehicle for data source. Mitigation: Define strict telemetry schema and buffering contract; Vehicle owns sensor hardware, Safety Services owns fusion logic. Risk level: Low (acceptable boundary diffusion—data flow, not logic duplication).

Tasking interface diffusion: Policy-aware feature queries are consumed by Teleoperation and Vehicle for mission planning. Safety Services exposes results but does not control consumption. Mitigation: Define query response schema and policy annotation format; consumers must not replicate filtering logic locally. Risk level: Medium (potential for local caching/replication if not enforced).

Policy update synchronization: Operational policy updates flow inbound via Tasking interface. Safety Services must apply updates atomically to avoid inconsistent feature filtering. Mitigation: Implement versioned policy application with rollback capability; validate policy updates before applying to active queries. Risk level: Medium (consistency risk if updates race with active queries).

Spatial graph state consistency: If distributed architecture is required (Network Link interface), bidirectional synchronization of spatial graph and constraint traceability must be maintained. Mitigation: Define eventual consistency model or strong consistency protocol; monitor for stale references. Risk level: High (only if distributed; low if centralized).

Local caching diffusion: Vehicle or Teleoperation modules may cache spatial features locally for performance optimization, creating divergent copies and consistency issues. Mitigation: Enforce strict ownership of spatial graph by Safety Services; provide efficient query interface to minimize caching incentive. Risk level: High (critical to prevent).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Containment score 0.92 is justified. Safety Services is the natural, cohesive home for the integrated DP. All core logic (sensor fusion, graph representation, safety attribute binding, constraint traceability, policy-aware filtering) is co-located and independently testable. The 0.08 diffusion score reflects only acceptable boundary data flow (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert out). No logic duplication or scattered responsibilities detected. Alternative allocations (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) would scatter safety-critical reasoning and violate separation of concerns. Recommendation: Proceed with Safety Services as the IM boundary; implement mitigations for identified diffusion risks, particularly local caching prevention and policy update synchronization.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Telemetry interface contract: Define multi-modal sensor stream schema, buffering requirements, and latency SLAs. Specify which sensor modalities are mandatory vs. optional for spatial feature capture.

Implement policy update versioning and atomic application: Add version identifiers to operational policies; ensure all active queries use consistent policy version; provide rollback mechanism for failed policy updates.

Establish spatial graph ownership enforcement: Document that Safety Services is the sole owner of spatial graph state; prohibit local caching in Vehicle or Teleoperation without explicit Safety Services coordination; provide efficient query interface to minimize caching incentive.

Define bidirectional traceability data structure: Specify how features link to safety plan constraints and vice versa; ensure no circular dependencies; document reference lifecycle (creation, update, invalidation).

Add memory and query latency monitoring: Specify acceptable bounds for spatial graph size, query response time, and constraint evaluation latency; implement monitoring and alerting for Safety Services.

Clarify Network Link interface scope: If distributed architecture is required, define synchronization protocol (eventual consistency vs. strong consistency), conflict resolution strategy, and network partition handling.

Document acceptance criteria verification plan: Map each acceptance criterion to specific test cases (e.g., sensor simulation for model generation, interface contract tests for availability, traceability audit for safety plan binding).

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-DP-IM alignment with high verification feasibility and excellent allocation clarity. However, diffusion risks are non-trivial (Medium-High severity on policy synchronization, local caching prevention, and distributed state consistency), and the recommended revisions expose critical sub-functional dependencies that must be decomposed to ensure safety-critical correctness. The integrated DP is logically sound but operationally complex; decomposition into sub-FRs will make explicit the functional requirements for policy management, state consistency, and interface contracts that are currently implicit in the DP. Given high safety criticality, proceeding to next-level decomposition is justified to surface and allocate these concerns explicitly.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Capture multi-modal sensor streams (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) from Vehicle telemetry interface and buffer them with defined latency SLAs.
FR1.2: Fuse multi-modal sensor data into a unified coordinate frame and validate fusion output against sensor reliability thresholds.
FR1.3: Construct and maintain a structured spatial graph representing worksite geometry, feature classification, and safety-critical attributes.
FR1.4: Bind spatial features to worksite safety plan constraints via bidirectional traceability and enforce constraint lifecycle management (creation, update, invalidation).
FR1.5: Ingest, version, and atomically apply operational policy updates to active spatial feature queries without inconsistency or race conditions.
FR1.6: Expose spatial features and safety attributes via a policy-aware query interface that filters results according to current operational policy version.
FR1.7: Prevent unauthorized local caching or replication of spatial graph state in Vehicle or Teleoperation modules; enforce Safety Services as sole owner of spatial state.
FR1.8: If distributed architecture is required, synchronize spatial graph and constraint traceability across network boundaries with defined consistency model and conflict resolution.
FR1.9: Monitor spatial graph memory footprint, query response latency, and constraint evaluation performance; alert on SLA violations.

revise.why: The current integrated DP (DP1 + DP5) is logically complete but operationally underspecified. Decomposition into sub-FRs will require corresponding sub-DPs that explicitly address: (1) sensor buffering and latency contracts (FR1.1); (2) fusion validation logic (FR1.2); (3) spatial graph data structure and update semantics (FR1.3); (4) traceability binding and lifecycle management (FR1.4); (5) policy versioning and atomic application (FR1.5); (6) query filtering and policy enforcement (FR1.6); (7) ownership enforcement mechanisms (FR1.7); (8) distributed synchronization protocol (FR1.8); (9) monitoring and alerting (FR1.9). These sub-DPs will make explicit the architectural decisions and interface contracts that are currently implicit, reducing diffusion risk and improving verifiability. Recommend revising DP to decompose into sub-DPs aligned with sub-FRs before proceeding to detailed design.

assumptions: Safety Services module has sufficient computational capacity to perform real-time multi-modal sensor fusion and spatial graph maintenance without exceeding latency SLAs.
Vehicle module provides telemetry interface with defined schema, buffering, and reliability guarantees; Safety Services does not own sensor hardware.
Operational policies are well-formed, versioned, and provided via Tasking interface; Safety Services is responsible for atomic application but not policy authorship.
Worksite safety plan constraints are static or change infrequently; constraint updates are coordinated through formal change control process.
Teleoperation and Vehicle modules will not cache spatial features locally if Safety Services provides efficient query interface; caching is not required for performance.
If distributed architecture is required, network latency and partition tolerance are acceptable; eventual consistency model is sufficient for spatial feature representation.
Acceptance criteria (model generation, availability, feature set agreement) are verifiable through simulation, interface testing, and traceability audit.
open_risks: Policy update synchronization race condition: Active queries may use inconsistent policy versions if updates are not applied atomically. Mitigation required: versioned policy application with rollback.

Local caching diffusion: Vehicle or Teleoperation modules may cache spatial features to optimize performance, creating divergent copies and consistency issues. Mitigation required: enforce ownership, provide efficient query interface, monitor for unauthorized caching.

Distributed state consistency: If Network Link interface is used, bidirectional synchronization of spatial graph and constraint traceability must be maintained. Mitigation required: define consistency model, conflict resolution, and network partition handling.

Sensor fusion validation: Multi-modal sensor fusion may produce inconsistent or unreliable spatial features if sensor reliability thresholds are not enforced. Mitigation required: define fusion validation logic and sensor reliability contracts.
Spatial graph memory footprint: Spatial graph may grow unbounded if old features are not pruned or archived. Mitigation required: define feature lifecycle and retention policy.

Query latency SLA: Policy-aware query interface may exceed latency SLAs if constraint evaluation is inefficient. Mitigation required: optimize query execution, define acceptable latency bounds, monitor performance.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: Define multi-modal sensor stream schema, buffering requirements, latency SLAs, and sensor reliability metrics.
Sensor fusion validation logic: Specify fusion algorithm, reliability thresholds, and validation criteria for multi-modal sensor data.
Spatial graph data structure specification: Define graph representation, feature attributes, geometry encoding, and update semantics.
Traceability binding specification: Define bidirectional links between spatial features and safety plan constraints; specify reference lifecycle and consistency rules.
Policy versioning and atomic application specification: Define policy version identifiers, atomic application semantics, rollback mechanism, and consistency guarantees.
Query interface specification: Define query language, filtering semantics, policy annotation format, and response schema.
Ownership enforcement mechanism: Document how Safety Services prevents unauthorized caching in Vehicle or Teleoperation; specify monitoring and enforcement.
Distributed synchronization protocol (if required): Define consistency model, conflict resolution strategy, network partition handling, and synchronization latency bounds.
Monitoring and alerting specification: Define metrics (memory, latency, constraint evaluation time), acceptable bounds, and alerting thresholds.
Acceptance criteria verification plan: Map each acceptance criterion to specific test cases (sensor simulation, interface contract tests, traceability audit).
